topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy printout felt like a physical assault in my hand. Every line of the genetic report screamed a betrayal. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent light of the kitchen buzzing overhead reflecting in my wide, unblinking eyes. The results were undeniable: a match. A deep, undeniable connection to a family I had never known, a family that had chosen me, completely bypassing me. My jaw tightened so hard I thought my molars might crack. I crumpled the paper, the sound sharp in the silence of the apartment. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, breathing in the stale city air, trying to physically shrink the enormity of the information.

[story 2]

"You're telling me?" The words scraped out of my throat, sounding brittle and thin even to my own ears. I didn't look at Sarah; I couldn't bear to see the carefully constructed calm on her face. My hands were shaking, a low, vibrating tremor that I fought to suppress by clenching my fists until my knuckles were white. The data on the screen swam before my vision—names, dates, shared markers—each piece a shard of glass digging into my composure. I pushed back from the table, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. "This is a sham. A cruel joke." My voice was low, edged with a venom that surprised even me, a desperate attempt to build a wall between myself and the reality unfolding before me.

[story 3]

I kept replaying the sequence of the test in my mind, the statistical probabilities turning into a concrete, damning verdict. A coldness seeped into my bones, a deep chill that had nothing to do with the temperature of the room. When my mother finally spoke, her voice was soft, laced with an unbearable tenderness that only amplified my internal resistance. I simply turned away, my back rigid, refusing to acknowledge the warmth radiating from her. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the dial pad, not to call, but to stare at the screen, letting the silence stretch until it became unbearable. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to spring, fueled by a sheer, unadulterated refusal to accept this manufactured narrative.
